titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Julian Joel Obermann Papers
titlestmt.author: Mary A. Caldera

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

conventiondeclaration.abbr: Finding aid prepared according to Describing Archives: A Content Standard (DACS).

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday December 4, 2024 at 03:00
Finding aid revised to reflect arrangement and description of previously closed until processed material.

did.abstract: The papers consist of correspondence, draft manuscripts, teaching and committee files documenting the research, writing, editing, and teaching of Julian Joel Obermann, professor of Semitic languages at Yale University and editor of the Yale Judaica Series.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Closed pending completion of procssing in December 2024.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of the estate of Julian Obermann, 1959. Transfer from the holdings of the Yale Judaica Series, November, 1966.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Julian Joel Obermann (1888-1956) was born in Warsaw, Poland. He received his Ph.D., University of Vienna, 1915 and M.A. (honorary) from Yale University in 1935. Obermann emigrated to the United States in 1923. Obermann held several teaching positions: professor of Semitic languages, University of Hamburg, Germany (1919-1922?); professor of Semitic philology, Jewish Institute of Religion, New York, 1923-1931; professor, Hebrew University of Jerusalem, 1929-1930; visiting professor and professor of Semitic languages, Yale University, 1932-1956. Obermann served as co-editor of the Journal of Biblical Literature (1934-1938) and editor-in-chief of the Yale Judaica Series (1944-1956). Obermann specialized in semitic languages, Old Testament and Ugartic studies, and Islamic and Arabic culture and philosophy. He authored, edited and translated several works, including Das Problem der Kausalitaet bei den Arabern (1916); Der philosophische and religioese Subjektivismus Ghazalis (1921); The Arabic Original of Ibn Shahin's Book of Comfort (1933); Inscribed Tiles from the Synagogue of Dura (1942), Ugaritic Mythology (1948), Hugo Gressman's Tower of Babel (1928), and Soloman Gandz's translation of Maimonides' Sanctification of the New Moon (1956).
Obermann never married. He died in New Haven, Connecticut, on October 17, 1956 at the age of 68.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Julian Joel Obermann Papers (MS 330). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The papers consist of correspondence, lectures, presentations, writings, teaching and committee files documenting the research, writing, editing, and teaching of Julian Joel Obermann, professor of Semitic languages at Yale University and editor of the Yale Oriental Series and the Yale Judaica Series. The correspondence is primarily incoming and is both personal and professional in nature. Most of the lectures, presentations, and writings are drafts, identified and unidentified, and range from partial to complete manuscripts. The writings document Obermann's writing and editing projects, including The Arabic Original of Ibn Shahin's Book of Comfort (1933); Inscribed Tiles from the Synagogue of Dura (1942), Ugaritic Mythology (1948), Gressman's Tower of Babel (1928), The Code of Maimonides (1948-1949), and Gandz's Maimonides' Sanctification of the New Moon (1956). The bulk of the papers consist of Obermann's notebooks, which he kept for much of his career. The notebooks contain manuscript writings, lectures, and editorial and research notes. Most are not identified and have not been organized or individually described. Files on the American Oriental Society, the Committee on Judaica Research, the Judaica Research Committee, the Semitic and Biblical Club (Yale), and the Near East Languages and Literature Department (Yale), though few in number, document Obermann's leadership and interest in promoting semitic and Judaica studies. The papers also include a few legal documents, photographs, and newspaper clippings.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright status for collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.
